title: Indefinite Causal Order Reverses the Real-Complex Hierarchy
authors: Jacopo Surace, Shintaro Minagawa, Ravi Kunjwal
abstract: Can causal relations be subject to quantum indefiniteness, similar to other physical properties? The process-matrix framework formalises this possibility: valid processes are defined by what local laboratories can implement, without assuming a global causal order. Standardly, the local labs are assumed to implement arbitrary quantum instruments. We ask what happens when symmetries restrict these local operations. Symmetry constraints, such as those arising from missing reference frames, superselection constraints, or the antiunitary symmetry defining real quantum theory, enlarge the admissible process cone. Do these extra processes generate genuinely new correlations? We prove a sharp dichotomy: no for any finite unitary symmetry, yes for real quantum theory. Recent work has shown that, under fixed and definite causal order, complex quantum theory is strictly richer than real quantum theory. Our work shows that this hierarchy is reversed under indefinite causal order: real quantum theory realizes strictly more process correlations than complex quantum theory.
summary: This theoretical paper explores how quantum correlations are constrained by the assumed causal order and the underlying mathematical structure of quantum theory. It proves that when causality is indefinite, the physical constraints of Real Quantum Theory allow for strictly more correlations than those predicted by standard Complex Quantum Theory. This reversal of the complexity hierarchy is a key finding in quantum process theory.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether causal relations can be subject to quantum indefiniteness and compares the operational distinction between real and complex quantum theories under such indefinite causal order.
main_result: Under indefinite causal order, Real Quantum Theory (RQT) realizes strictly more process correlations than standard Complex Quantum Theory (QT), reversing the hierarchy observed for fixed causal orders.
method: The analysis uses the process-matrix framework to characterize achievable process correlations under symmetry constraints, comparing those derived from unitary (Twirled) and antiunitary (Real) symmetries.
model_or_system: The study models quantum processes using process matrices in finite-dimensional bipartite qubit systems, comparing the constraints imposed by complex quantum theory (QT) versus real quantum theory (RQT).
key_observables: The set of achievable process correlations ($	ext{Cproc,fin}$), the Lazy Guess Your Neighbour’s Input (LGYNI) causal inequality, and the structure of the process matrix in the Pauli basis.
important_parameters: Finite dimensionality of the process matrices; the nature of the symmetry constraint (unitary vs. antiunitary).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to finite-dimensional process correlations and relies on the operational definition provided by the process-matrix framework.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the hierarchy of correlations across different causal assumptions (definite vs. indefinite) and compare the constraints imposed by different symmetries.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing the equivalence of QT correlations with those from twirled theories, then proving that the antiunitary symmetry (RQT) imposes strictly weaker constraints, leading to the reversal of the QT/RQT hierarchy under indefinite causality.
why_interesting: This work provides a deep, operational understanding of how fundamental assumptions about physical symmetries (like reality constraints) can fundamentally alter the correlations achievable in quantum systems, especially when causality itself is fuzzy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Real–complex hierarchy across causal assumptions. For fixed definite multipartite causal structures, QT is strictly richer than RQT [4]. For bipartite definite-order correlations, RQT and QT coincide [7, 8]. For process correlations with indefinite causal order, the hierarchy reverses: RQT is strictly richer than QT, as shown in this work. The point p⋆denotes the separating correlation constructed here.
